messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: Ok, I did the swap with the EByte 30S to the 22S. Everything is the same except the module. I went from the MeshAdv to MeshAdvMini. The same raspi, same software, same PoE, etc. The noise floor with the 30S was around -78 and with the 22S it's more like -110. My advice is to either not get the 30S or do something like bypass the LNA. I don't think it's doing anyone any favors.
messages.content_html: Ok, I did the swap with the EByte 30S to the 22S. Everything is the same except the module. I went from the MeshAdv to MeshAdvMini. The same raspi, same software, same PoE, etc. The noise floor with the 30S was around -78 and with the 22S it's more like -110. My advice is to either not get the 30S or do something like bypass the LNA. I don't think it's doing anyone any favors.
messages.reply_html:
hpux735
I want to keep as much the same as possible. If I switch the hat from the 30S to the 22S with everything else the same, it'll be very informative

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: I think the idea of an "LNA" specifically is it's supposed to have more immunity to noise than a standard amplifier. Indeed, the "Y method" of testing the noise figure of an LNA is specifically by injecting noise into it and seeing how it responds

messages.author: Fish
messages.text: Ok here is the results of the Ebyte E22 Lora Module LNA removal . Both hardware sets, antennas, test locations and proximity to companion node are identical (the tests were carried out with only one unit running at a time). One board has an E22 with original LNA and other has it removed internally. The pcb with LNA removed has a rock solid noise floor of -114 whereas the one with original LNA retained floats around -99 to -102. The other difference in the test case is the RSSI is slightly worse on the one with LNA removed versus the one with given the fixed proximity to the companion node. If anyone is considering doing this it appears to yield a positive result overall for improved noise floor. (edited)
messages.content_html: Ok here is the results of the Ebyte E22 Lora Module LNA removal . Both hardware sets, antennas, test locations and proximity to companion node are identical (the tests were carried out with only one unit running at a time). One board has an E22 with original LNA and other has it removed internally. The pcb with LNA removed has a rock solid noise floor of -114 whereas the one with original LNA retained floats around -99 to -102. The other difference in the test case is the RSSI is slightly worse on the one with LNA removed versus the one with given the fixed proximity to the companion node. If anyone is considering doing this it appears to yield a positive result overall for improved noise floor. (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 1

Then I've got a bit ol' stack of attenuators and I'm going to stop the signal down enough to have a measurable rate of packet loss. Then you'll be able to really explore the space of marginal signal
messages.content_html: Exactly, Andrew. I'm working on a test for this actually. I just got a farraday bag, and I hoping I can attenuate the outside world with it enough. messages.reactions_html: 2
messages.reply_html:
AG / Andrew
To test this you can't look at the noise floor. While it's a good indicator of how much noise the radio hears it ultimately does not determine whether you can receive well or not. A better test is to have a node you are receiving with a weak signal... Then compare the SNR on both the LNA node and LNA removed node.

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: I honestly thought it would be easier to do this experiment. It turns out it's actually incredibly difficult to get more than 100dB of effective isolation. I put one V4 in an enclosed aluminum enclosure with a tight-fitting bulkhead N-connector. I even made a gasket out of aluminum foil. With a dummy load on the N-connector, I could still get pings through. So, I wrapped the whole thing in aluminum foil. I could still get pings through. So I wrapped the N-connector SMA adapter and dummy load in aluminum foil. Finally I stopped getting pings. Ok, so I added a right-angle and put 90dB of attenuation on it and put the dummy load on the end of that. Pings started going through again. So, I wrap that whole thing in aluminum foil. I stop getting pings again. I added another 12dB (total of 102dB) and the dummy load, and I could get pings through. Basically, it's possible to block enough RF to stop getting pings through, but it'll never be repeatable enough. I just get get the signal small enough that I can controllably re-add it. Once I made measurements, and I take the V4 out to bypass the LNA (this is just an example, I'd do this with the E22-900M30S because that's the subject of the debate) it would completely invalidate the whole experiment. (edited)
messages.content_html: I honestly thought it would be easier to do this experiment. It turns out it's actually incredibly difficult to get more than 100dB of effective isolation. I put one V4 in an enclosed aluminum enclosure with a tight-fitting bulkhead N-connector. I even made a gasket out of aluminum foil. With a dummy load on the N-connector, I could still get pings through. So, I wrapped the whole thing in aluminum foil. I could still get pings through. So I wrapped the N-connector SMA adapter and dummy load in aluminum foil. Finally I stopped getting pings. Ok, so I added a right-angle and put 90dB of attenuation on it and put the dummy load on the end of that. Pings started going through again. So, I wrap that whole thing in aluminum foil. I stop getting pings again. I added another 12dB (total of 102dB) and the dummy load, and I could get pings through. Basically, it's possible to block enough RF to stop getting pings through, but it'll never be repeatable enough. I just get get the signal small enough that I can controllably re-add it. Once I made measurements, and I take the V4 out to bypass the LNA (this is just an example, I'd do this with the E22-900M30S because that's the subject of the debate) it would completely invalidate the whole experiment. (edited)

messages.author: mrdan1023
messages.text: Interesting to follow your experimentation with this module. I have been using E22P-915M30S modules which are similar but with a SAW filter in the RX path. I had also been trying to measure the low signal performance of several brand modules but run into the same problems with RF leakage and gave up. An unrelated test I did the other day might have a suitable solution. I was using a UHF module to transmit, with an external mixer/siggen/attenuation I could place the resulting signal wherever I needed without transmitting on the receive frequency. I used an old crystal detector for the mixer, drove it with about 10dBm from a sig gen. I plan to come back to the E22P-915M30S and remove the LNA, then put it through a bunch of tests looking at sensitivity and noise floor. Ultimately simulating overload conditions from mobile phone base station and trying to determine if the SAW filter is effective at helping avoid the problem.

Ultimately simulating overload conditions from mobile phone base station and trying to determine if the SAW filter is effective at helping avoid the problem.
